subtitle: In the witness box of empire, a calm voice weighs duty against dominion

panels.shot: low-angle medium close-up in a dim colonial courtroom
panels.action: Bal Gangadhar Tilak stands in the witness box of a grand British-era Bombay courtroom, calm and upright in traditional attire, British judges looming in shadowed distance, onlookers crowding the gallery benches.

dialogue.text: Bombay, 1908. In the hushed hall of a colonial court, Bal Gangadhar Tilak lifts his voice, and the idea of swaraj stands trial with him.

i18n.hi: बॉम्बे, 1908। सूनी अंग्रेज़ी अदालत में बाल गंगाधर तिलक की आवाज़ उठती है, और उनके साथ ही स्वराज कटघरे में खड़ा है।

panels.shot: wide establishing shot from balcony height
panels.action: A panoramic view of early 1900s Bombay shows dusty roads, tram lines, mill chimneys and crowded bazaars under a hazy sky, with the imposing façade of the High Court rising over the harbor city.

dialogue.text: By 1908, Bombay is a restless city. Mills roar, trams rattle, and beneath the drone of empire, talk of nationalism moves from whisper to word.

i18n.hi: 1908 तक बॉम्बे बेचैन है। मिलें गरजती हैं, ट्राम खड़खड़ाती हैं, और साम्राज्य के शोर के नीचे फुसफुसाहट से बात तक, राष्ट्रवाद फैल रहा है।

panels.shot: medium shot in a modest newspaper office interior
panels.action: Bal Gangadhar Tilak sits at a wooden desk in a cluttered vernacular press office, proofs and newspapers of Kesari and The Mahratta scattered, as a compositor waits and a ceiling fan turns lazily above.

dialogue.text: Bal Gangadhar Tilak, teacher and editor, has turned his pen into a chisel. In Kesari and The Mahratta he cuts at indifference, educating a people starved of power.

i18n.hi: बाल गंगाधर तिलक, अध्यापक और सम्पादक, ने कलम को छैनी बना लिया है। केसरी और द मराठा में वो बेहोशी चीरते हैं, ताकत से वंचितों को पढ़ाते हैं।

panels.shot: over-the-shoulder close-up on newspaper columns
panels.action: From behind Tilak’s shoulder, sharply inked Marathi columns are visible on a Kesari proof, with a bold editorial headline criticizing arbitrariness and calling readers to political awareness, as Tilak’s hand rests on the page.

dialogue.text: His editorials strike at deportations without trial, at laws bent to crush protest. The government calls his words sedition. He calls them duty.

i18n.hi: उनके लेख निर्वासन, काले क़ानून और कुचली गई आवाज़ों पर वार करते हैं। सरकार इसे राजद्रोह कहती है, वो इसे अपना फ़र्ज़ कहते हैं।

panels.shot: dramatic medium wide in the newsroom doorway
panels.action: Two British police officers in stiff uniforms and a Parsi court officer stand at the doorway of Tilak’s office, warrant in hand, as Tilak rises from his chair, face composed, staff looking on in alarm.

dialogue.text: In June 1908, after new bombings shake the Raj, officials turn again to a familiar name. A warrant comes, not for a bomb-maker, but for a writer.

i18n.hi: जून 1908, नए धमाकों से राज दहलता है, और अफ़सरों को एक पुराना नाम याद आता है। वारंट आता है—बम बनाने वाले के लिए नहीं, लिखने वाले के लिए।

panels.shot: profile medium shot on the steps of the court
panels.action: Tilak is escorted up the wide stone steps of the Bombay High Court between guards, an anxious crowd of Indians and Europeans watching, some with folded hands, some whispering, the Gothic arches looming above.

dialogue.text: Charged under Section 124A for inciting disaffection, Tilak walks up the High Court steps. For him, this is no surprise. He has stood here before, and may stand here again.

i18n.hi: धारा 124A के तहत राजद्रोह भड़काने का आरोप। तिलक हाई कोर्ट की सीढ़ियाँ नापते हैं। उन्हें कोई हैरत नहीं; वो पहले भी आए हैं, फिर आ सकते हैं।

panels.shot: high wide shot from the back of the courtroom
panels.action: A packed colonial courtroom stretches before us, British judges on a high bench, a European jury seated in a row, barristers in gowns at their tables, and Tilak seated beside his counsel amid a sea of spectators.

dialogue.text: The 1908 trial begins before a special jury, mostly European, under the shadow of the previous conviction of 1897. The air is thick with heat, and with foregone conclusions.

i18n.hi: 1908 का मुक़दमा ख़ास ज्यूरी के सामने शुरू होता है, ज्यादातर यूरोपीय, 1897 की सज़ा की परछाईं में। हवा गरम है, और फैसले लगभग तय।

panels.shot: medium shot on the judges’ bench angled toward Tilak
panels.action: A stern British judge in robes leans forward on the bench, clerk beside him with notes, as he addresses the court; in the foreground, Tilak listens, hands folded, expression steady.

dialogue.text: The Crown reads passages from Kesari, insisting that metaphors of fire and oppression are calls to violence. To criticize government, they argue, is to sap its ‘lawful authority’.

i18n.hi: ताज केसरी से अंश पढ़ता है। वो कहते हैं, आग और जुल्म की उपमाएँ हिंसा के उकसावे हैं। हुकूमत की आलोचना, उनके मुताबिक, उसके ‘कानूनी अधिकार’ को खोखला करना है।

panels.shot: medium close-up on Tilak and his counsel at the defense table
panels.action: Tilak sits beside his advocate at the defense table, head slightly bowed as they consult a sheaf of notes, with law books piled nearby and the gaze of the jury falling upon him.

dialogue.text: Tilak’s counsel presses that criticism of officials is not hatred of the state, and that a people kept voiceless must speak through their press. But in this court, law bends toward empire.

i18n.hi: तिलक के वकील कहते हैं, अफ़सरों की आलोचना राज्य से नफ़रत नहीं होती, और जिनके मुँह पर ताला है, वो अख़बार से बोलेंगे ही। पर इस अदालत में क़ानून, राज के तरफ़ झुकता है।

panels.shot: tight close-up on Tilak’s face as he rises
panels.action: Tilak is shown from the chest up as he rises to address the court, face calm yet grave, eyes fixed ahead, the blurred outlines of judge and jury visible behind him.

dialogue.text: When asked if he will speak, Tilak stands. This is no orator’s stage, no Ganapati pandal. It is the witness box of an empire. Yet his voice is steady.

i18n.hi: जब पूछा जाता है, वो बोलेंगे या नहीं, तिलक उठते हैं। न ये मंच है, न गणपति का पंडाल—ये साम्राज्य का गवाह-खाना है। फिर भी आवाज़ एकदम ठोस।

panels.shot: medium shot from jury’s perspective
panels.action: Seen from the jury box, Tilak stands in the witness area, one hand resting lightly on the rail, addressing the bench and jury, with Indian spectators leaning forward tensely in the background.

dialogue.text: He tells the court he has no personal grievance, only a public duty. If his writings have stirred unrest, it is because injustice breeds unrest, not because he wishes for violence.

i18n.hi: वो कहते हैं, उन्हें कोई निजी शिकवा नहीं, बस सार्वजनिक ज़िम्मेदारी है। अगर उनके लेखों से बेचैनी फैली है, तो इसलिए कि ज़ुल्म बेचैनी पैदा करता है, न कि उन्हें ख़ून चाहिए।

panels.shot: close-up on Tilak’s hands resting on the dock railing
panels.action: Tilak’s age-lined hands grip the polished wooden rail of the dock, knuckles firm yet relaxed, as if anchoring himself between two worlds, the blurred hint of the union jack behind.

dialogue.text: He reminds the judges that loyalty cannot be commanded by fear. A subject may obey, he says, but a citizen must consent. His conscience will not be bought by safety.

i18n.hi: वो जजों को याद दिलाते हैं, वफ़ादारी डर से नहीं खरीदी जा सकती। राया बस मान सकता है, नागरिक को राज़ी होना पड़ता है। उनकी आत्मा सुरक्षा के बदले बिकने वाली नहीं।

panels.shot: medium close-up, slight low angle on Tilak as he finishes speaking
panels.action: With a slight uplift of his chin, Tilak concludes his statement before the silent court, eyes clear and jaw set, a faint sheen of heat on his forehead matching the tense stillness around him.

dialogue.speaker: bal_gangadhar_tilak
dialogue.text: In matters of public duty, I must act according to my conscience.

i18n.hi: सार्वजनिक फ़र्ज़ में मुझे अपनी अंतरात्मा की आवाज़ ही माननी होगी।

panels.shot: wide shot capturing judge, jury, and crowded gallery
panels.action: The judge leans forward to address the jury while they sit impassive; the Indian gallery is packed, with men in turbans and caps straining to hear, some eyes wet, others burning with contained anger.

dialogue.text: The summing up leaves little doubt. The judge warns the jury of the ‘dangerous tendency’ of such writings. In the galleries, hearts sink, but no one moves. They have seen such trials before.

i18n.hi: जज का बयान सब साफ़ कर देता है। वो ज्यूरी को ऐसे लेखों की ‘ख़तरनाक प्रवृत्ति’ से डराते हैं। दीर्घाओं में दिल बैठते हैं, पर कोई हिलता नहीं; ऐसे मुक़दमे वो देख चुके हैं।

panels.shot: medium shot on the jury delivering verdict, Tilak in foreground
panels.action: A European juror rises, verdict paper in hand, while Tilak stands motionless in the foreground, profile calm, as the courtroom watches the moment that will decide his fate.

dialogue.text: The verdict comes: guilty of sedition. The word is simple, the weight enormous. A man is condemned, and with him, they hope to condemn a rising impatience.

i18n.hi: फ़ैसला आता है: राजद्रोह साबित। शब्द छोटा, वज़न भारी। एक आदमी को सज़ा मिलती है, और उसके साथ उठती बेक़रारी को भी बाँधने की कोशिश।

panels.shot: dramatic low-angle medium on Tilak as sentence is pronounced
panels.action: From a low angle, Tilak stands straight in the dock while the unseen judge pronounces a harsh sentence, his figure framed by the high ceiling and colonial pillars, as a few Indians in the gallery cover their faces.

dialogue.text: Six years’ transportation to Mandalay in Burma. The court hopes distance will erase influence, that iron bars and sea water will cool an overheated land.

i18n.hi: छह साल के लिए बर्मा के मंडाले भेजने का हुक्म। अदालत को उम्मीद है दूरी से असर मिट जाएगा, लोहे की सलाखें और समंदर इस तपती ज़मीन को ठंडा कर देंगे।

panels.shot: close-up on Tilak’s serene face after sentencing
panels.action: Tilak’s face fills the frame, expression serene but tired, eyes reflecting both sorrow and resolve, as faint light from a high window falls across his features.

dialogue.text: Tilak bows slightly. He accepts the sentence, but not the charge. In his silence now there is the same challenge as in his words: that law without justice is only power.

i18n.hi: तिलक हल्का सा झुकते हैं। सज़ा मानते हैं, इल्ज़ाम नहीं। उनकी खामोशी में भी वही चुनौती है: जो क़ानून न्याय के बिना हो, वो बस नंगा ज़ोर है।

panels.shot: medium shot outside the court as Tilak is led to a waiting carriage
panels.action: Outside the High Court, Tilak is escorted toward a waiting horse-drawn prison van, an Indian crowd pressing against a ring of police, some raising their hands in respectful salutation as he passes.

dialogue.text: As he is led away, the people outside do not see a defeated man. They see a teacher punished for teaching, a leader who has chosen prison over silence.

i18n.hi: जब वो ले जाए जाते हैं, बाहर की भीड़ किसी हारे हुए को नहीं देखती। उन्हें दिखता है एक गुरु, जिसे पढ़ाने की सज़ा मिली है, वो नेता जिसने चुप्पी से बेहतर जेल चुनी।

panels.shot: wide shot at the docks with the ship in the background
panels.action: On the Bombay docks, under a hazy sun, Tilak stands guarded near a gangplank leading to a steamer bound east, cranes and cargo silhouetted behind, as distant city roofs fade into the mist.

dialogue.text: From Bombay to Mandalay, the journey begins. In the privacy of his mind he turns again to books, to scriptures, to history. Even in exile, he prepares to teach anew.

i18n.hi: बॉम्बे से मंडाले तक सफ़र शुरू होता है। भीतर ही भीतर वो फिर किताबों, धर्मग्रंथों, इतिहास की ओर लौटते हैं। वनवास में भी वो अगला पाठ तैयार करते हैं।

panels.shot: interior medium shot of a simple prison cell in Mandalay
panels.action: In a sparse Mandalay prison cell, Tilak sits cross-legged on a mat with a thick book open on his lap, a small barred window casting a rectangle of light onto the worn floor.

dialogue.text: In confinement he studies and writes, wrestling with the Gita and with politics, thinking of how a nation can be taught to think of itself as a nation.

i18n.hi: क़ैद में वो पढ़ते और लिखते हैं, गीता और राजनीति से जूझते हैं, सोचते हैं, एक कौम को कैसे सिखाया जाए कि वो खुद को राष्ट्र समझे।

panels.shot: montage-style wide shot of Indian streets hearing the news
panels.action: A composite street scene shows peasants around a village newspaper reader, clerks in a city chawl sharing a small English paper, and students in a verandah reading about Tilak’s sentence, all reacting with a mix of shock and resolve.

dialogue.text: Across India, news of the trial spreads. For some, it is their first clear lesson in the cost of speaking truth. For others, it is the moment they quietly choose the path of politics.

i18n.hi: पूरे भारत में मुक़दमे की ख़बर फैलती है। किसी के लिए ये सच बोलने की कीमत का पहला साफ़ सबक है, किसी के लिए यही पल है जब वो चुपचाप राजनीति की राह चुनते हैं।

panels.shot: symbolic low-angle shot of an empty courtroom with Tilak’s shadow overlay
panels.action: The empty Bombay High Court courtroom is shown, benches vacant and dust motes in the air, while an imagined shadowed outline of Tilak stands near the dock, larger than life against the far wall.

dialogue.text: The British state wins its verdict, but not its argument. Tilak’s calm words in that crowded court teach a generation that courage before authority can be as powerful as rebellion in the streets.

i18n.hi: ब्रिटिश राज को फ़ैसला मिल जाता है, तर्क नहीं। भीड़ भरी अदालत में तिलक की ठंडी, साफ़ आवाज़ एक पीढ़ी को सिखाती है कि हुक्मरानों के सामने साहस सड़क पर बग़ावत जितना ताकतवर हो सकता है।

subtitle_i18n.en: In the witness box of empire, a calm voice weighs duty against dominion
subtitle_i18n.hi: साम्राज्य के गवाह-खाने में, एक शांत आवाज़ फ़र्ज़ और फ़तह का हिसाब लगाती है
